schools.summary: The LORD speaking concerning the sending of the spies: 'and the LORD spake unto Moses, saying' — the LORD speaking to Moses, now concerning the sending of the spies to search the land of Canaan (ch. 13, the speaking, God's word, the new section, the occasion, after the camp came to Paran, at the border of the land, now the sending of the spies, by God's permission given in answer to the people's desire, Deuteronomy 1:22, the figure, the sending of the spies introduced, the trial of faith at the door of the land, the truth that the spies were sent at the people's request, God permitting what their unbelief desired, the great turning point, the land in view, the test of faith at hand, God speaking concerning the spies, showing his hand even in the trial that would expose the people's unbelief); the LORD speaking to Moses (the sending of the spies, by permission, Deuteronomy 1:22) — the new section (the spies); the LORD spake unto Moses — the sending of the spies introduced (the providence, God permitting the spies, the trial of faith) — Henry, Gill, Calvin, Keil.

themes: And the LORD spake unto Moses, saying
the LORD speaking concerning the sending of the spies
God permitting the spies sent at the people's desire (Deuteronomy 1:22)
the trial of faith at the door of the land

schools.summary: The command to send men to search the land God had given: 'send thou men, that they may search the land of Canaan, which I give unto the children of Israel: of every tribe of their fathers shall ye send a man, every one a ruler among them' — the command — 'send thou men, that they may search the land of Canaan, which I give unto the children of Israel': God bid Moses send men, one ruler from each tribe, to search the land of Canaan, the land he was giving them (the witnesses noting the significance — the sending of the spies, by God's permission, in answer to the people's request, Deuteronomy 1:22, where the people first proposed it, God allowed it, though it became the occasion of their unbelief, the lesson — the trial of faith at the very door of the land, the land already given by God's promise, 'which I give unto the children of Israel' — needing no spying out, for God had promised it, yet the people, not content with the promise, would search it out, and the search became the test that exposed their unbelief, the tension — God's gift sure by promise, yet the people leaning to sight rather than faith, the figure — the danger of leaning to sight where God has given a promise, the trial that exposes whether the heart rests on God's word, the truth that 'the land which I give' was theirs by promise, and the spying out, though permitted, was the seedbed of the unbelief that followed, the figure of faith tested at the threshold of the promise, whether the people would believe God's word or walk by sight, the figure of the trial of faith at the door of the inheritance); the men sent to search the land, one ruler per tribe (the spies, by permission, Deuteronomy 1:22) — the sending (the search of the land); 'which I give unto the children of Israel: of every tribe of their fathers shall ye send a man, every one a ruler among them' (the land already given by promise, the chosen rulers sent, the land sure, the search the test of faith) — the figure (the trial of faith at the door, the leaning to sight, the providence, the spies chosen, the search to follow, vv.3-25) — Calvin, Keil, Wesley, Ellicott, Gill.

debates.question: Why were the spies sent to search a land God had already given?

sides.position: The search was permitted in answer to the people's request (Deuteronomy 1:22) - the land was already theirs by God's promise, needing no spying out; the trial at the door of the inheritance exposed whether the people rested on God's word or leaned to sight, and became the seedbed of their unbelief

themes: Send thou men, that they may search the land of Canaan, which I give unto the children of Israel: of every tribe of their fathers shall ye send a man, every one a ruler among them
the command to send men to search the land God had given (the crux of the trial of faith at the door of promise)
the search of the land already given by promise, permitted at the people's request (Deuteronomy 1:22)
the trial that would expose whether the people rested on God's word or leaned to sight

the great cluster of grapes from Eshcol
the great cluster so large it took two to carry, the abundant fruit the proof of the rich land
the earnest of the inheritance, the firstfruits of the promise (Deuteronomy 8:8)

schools.summary: The naming of Eshcol after the cluster: 'the place was called the brook Eshcol, because of the cluster of grapes which the children of Israel cut down from thence' — the valley was named Eshcol ('cluster') because of the great cluster cut there (the the place was called the brook Eshcol, the naming, the because of the cluster of grapes, the reason, the great cluster, the figure, the place named for the great fruit, a memorial of the land's richness, the name Eshcol a standing witness to the goodness of the land God had given, the truth that the very name preserved the evidence of the good land, the memorial of God's bounty, the figure of the abundant fruit of the inheritance, recalled in the name of the place); the valley named Eshcol (the memorial) — the name (Eshcol, 'cluster'); 'because of the cluster of grapes which the children of Israel cut down from thence' (the great cluster the reason for the name, the memorial of the land's richness, the figure, the name a witness to God's bounty, the providence, the place named, the return of the spies to follow, vv.25) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

themes: The place was called the brook Eshcol, because of the cluster of grapes which the children of Israel cut down from thence
the naming of Eshcol after the cluster
the valley named for the great cluster, a standing witness to the goodness of the land
the memorial of God's bounty

schools.summary: The return of the spies after forty days: 'and they returned from searching of the land after forty days' — the spies returned after forty days of searching (the they returned from searching of the land, the search completed, the after forty days, the period of the search, forty days, the figure, the forty days of the search, the period of testing, as forty is the number of trial in Scripture, the spies returning with the evidence, the trial of faith now to come to its crisis in the report, the truth that the forty days of searching would be answered by the forty years of wandering, a year for each day, vv.14:34, the figure of the period of trial, the search ended, the report and the great test of faith at hand); the spies returning (the search completed) — the return (the search ended); 'after forty days' (the forty days of the search, the period of trial, answered by forty years, Numbers 14:34, the figure, the number of testing, the providence, the spies returned, the report to follow, vv.26-33) — Calvin, Gill.

themes: And they returned from searching of the land after forty days
the return of the spies after forty days
the forty days of the search, the period of testing, answered later by the forty years of wandering
the report and the great test of faith now at hand (Numbers 14:34)

schools.summary: The spies bringing back word and the fruit to the congregation: 'and they went and came to Moses, and to Aaron, and to all the congregation of the children of Israel, unto the wilderness of Paran, to Kadesh; and brought back word unto them, and unto all the congregation, and shewed them the fruit of the land' — the spies came to Moses, Aaron, and the whole congregation at Kadesh, gave their report, and showed the fruit (the brought back word unto them, and unto all the congregation, the report given to all, the shewed them the fruit of the land, the fruit displayed as evidence, the figure, the report and the fruit set before the people, the evidence of the good land, the fruit a witness to the land's goodness, the proof of God's promise, yet the report would be mixed, the good land confessed but the obstacles magnified, the truth that the same evidence drew faith from some and unbelief from others, the fruit and the report set before all, the figure of the testimony to the inheritance, the proof of the promise displayed); the spies coming to all the congregation at Kadesh (the report to all) — the return (to the congregation); 'brought back word... and shewed them the fruit of the land' (the report given and the fruit shown, the evidence of the good land, the figure, the testimony to the inheritance, the proof displayed, the providence, the report and fruit set before all, the words to follow, vv.27-29) — Henry, Gill.

themes: And they went and came to Moses, and to Aaron, and to all the congregation of the children of Israel, unto the wilderness of Paran, to Kadesh; and brought back word unto them, and unto all the congregation, and shewed them the fruit of the land
the spies bringing back word and the fruit to the congregation
the report and the fruit set before all, the evidence of the good land
the proof of God's promise displayed, the same evidence to draw faith from some and unbelief from others

schools.summary: The confession that the land flowed with milk and honey: 'and they told him, and said, We came unto the land whither thou sentest us, and surely it floweth with milk and honey; and this is the fruit of it' — the spies confessed that the land flowed with milk and honey, as God had said, and showed the fruit (the surely it floweth with milk and honey, the confession of the good land, as God had promised, Exodus 3:8, the this is the fruit of it, the fruit as evidence, the figure, the good land confessed, the truth of God's promise acknowledged, the very fruit a witness that God had spoken truly, yet the confession would be followed by the fatal 'nevertheless,' vv.28, the good land owned but the obstacles set against it, the truth that the unbelieving may confess the goodness of God's promise and yet draw back from it, the figure of the good acknowledged but not embraced, the promise confessed but not believed); the good land confessed (flowing with milk and honey, Exodus 3:8) — the confession (the good land owned); 'this is the fruit of it' (the fruit the evidence, the proof of the good land, the figure, the promise confessed, soon to be doubted, vv.28, the providence, the good land owned, the 'nevertheless' to follow, vv.28) — Clarke, Gill.

themes: And they told him, and said, We came unto the land whither thou sentest us, and surely it floweth with milk and honey; and this is the fruit of it
the confession that the land flowed with milk and honey
the good land confessed, the truth of God's promise acknowledged (Exodus 3:8)
yet soon set against by the fatal 'nevertheless' of unbelief

schools.summary: The fatal 'nevertheless' of the strong people and walled cities: 'nevertheless the people be strong that dwell in the land, and the cities are walled, and very great: and moreover we saw the children of Anak there' — the spies, having confessed the good land, turned with the fatal 'nevertheless' to the strong people, the walled cities, and the giants (the Nevertheless, the turn from the promise to the obstacles, the word of unbelief, the the people be strong... the cities are walled, and very great, the formidable foe and defenses, the we saw the children of Anak there, the giants, the figure, the fatal 'nevertheless' that set the obstacles against the promise, the unbelief that, owning the good land, magnified the difficulties above God's power, the turn from faith to fear, the giants and walls set against the word of God, the truth that the same difficulties that the ten feared, Caleb and Joshua would face with faith, the obstacle the test of the heart, the figure of unbelief that looks at the giants and forgets the God who promised, 'we walk by faith, not by sight,' 2 Corinthians 5:7); the strong people and walled cities (the obstacles magnified) — the unbelief (the difficulties feared); 'we saw the children of Anak there' (the giants, the formidable foe, the obstacle, the figure, the unbelief that looks at the giants, 2 Corinthians 5:7, the providence, the obstacles set against the promise, Caleb's faith to follow, vv.30) — Ellicott, Gill.

themes: Nevertheless the people be strong that dwell in the land, and the cities are walled, and very great: and moreover we saw the children of Anak there
the fatal 'nevertheless' of the strong people and walled cities
the turn from the promise to the obstacles, the giants and walls magnified above God's power
the unbelief that looks at the difficulties and forgets the God who promised (2 Corinthians 5:7)

schools.summary: The enumeration of the peoples of the land: 'the Amalekites dwell in the land of the south: and the Hittites, and the Jebusites, and the Amorites, dwell in the mountains: and the Canaanites dwell by the sea, and by the coast of Jordan' — the spies named the many peoples of the land, set in every quarter (the the Amalekites... the Hittites... the Jebusites... the Amorites... the Canaanites, the many nations of the land, the dwell in the south... the mountains... by the sea... the coast of Jordan, the peoples in every region, the figure, the many enemies enumerated, the difficulties multiplied, the unbelief that counts the foes on every side, the obstacles named to magnify the fear, the truth that the same nations God had promised to drive out were now reckoned as too strong, the figure of unbelief that numbers the enemies and forgets the promise, the foes God would give into their hand, Exodus 23:23; Deuteronomy 7:1); the many nations named (the foes in every quarter, Deuteronomy 7:1) — the inhabitants (the peoples enumerated); 'the Canaanites dwell by the sea, and by the coast of Jordan' (the Canaanites in the lowlands, the foes on every side, the figure, the enemies God would drive out, Exodus 23:23, the providence, the peoples named, Caleb's faith to follow, vv.30) — Ellicott, Gill.

themes: The Amalekites dwell in the land of the south: and the Hittites, and the Jebusites, and the Amorites, dwell in the mountains: and the Canaanites dwell by the sea, and by the coast of Jordan
the enumeration of the peoples of the land
the many nations named in every quarter, the difficulties multiplied
the unbelief that counts the foes and forgets the God who promised to drive them out (Exodus 23:23; Deuteronomy 7:1)

schools.summary: Caleb's faith and his call to go up at once: 'and Caleb stilled the people before Moses, and said, Let us go up at once, and possess it; for we are well able to overcome it' — the call of faith — 'Let us go up at once, and possess it; for we are well able to overcome it': against the fearful report of the ten, Caleb stilled the people and called them to go up at once, confident they were well able (the witnesses noting the significance — the faith of Caleb, and Joshua with him, who, seeing the same giants and walls, yet trusted God and urged the people to go up, the lesson — the faith that rests on God and is not moved by the obstacles, Caleb saw the same difficulties as the ten, but looked beyond them to the God who had promised, the 'we are well able' not of presumption but of faith in God's power, 'if God be for us, who can be against us?' Romans 8:31, the courage of faith that stills the fearful and calls to action, the lone voice, with Joshua, against the unbelieving multitude, the figure — Caleb the man of faith, who 'wholly followed the LORD,' Numbers 14:24; 32:12; Joshua 14:8-9, a figure of the faithful who, against all the obstacles, trust God and press into the inheritance, the truth that faith looks not at the giants but at God, and is 'well able' through him, the figure of the believing few against the unbelieving many, the faith that overcomes the world, 1 John 5:4, the figure of the faith that rests on God and possesses the promise); Caleb stilling the fearful people (the lone voice of faith) — the stilling (the calming of the fear); 'Let us go up at once, and possess it; for we are well able to overcome it' (Caleb's confident call to go up, well able through God, the faith that rests on God, Romans 8:31; Numbers 14:24, the figure, the faith that overcomes, 1 John 5:4, the providence, Caleb's faith, the evil report of the ten to follow, vv.31-33) — Calvin, Ellicott, Wesley, Gill.

debates.question: What does Caleb's call to go up reveal about faith?

sides.position: The faith that, seeing the same giants and walls as the ten, yet looks beyond them to the God who promised - the 'we are well able' not of presumption but of confidence in God's power (Romans 8:31), the faith that wholly followed the LORD (Numbers 14:24) and overcomes the world (1 John 5:4)

themes: And Caleb stilled the people before Moses, and said, Let us go up at once, and possess it; for we are well able to overcome it
Caleb's faith and his call to go up at once (the crux of the faith that rests on God against the obstacles)
the faith that, seeing the same obstacles as the ten, yet trusted God and urged the people up (Numbers 14:24)
the 'we are well able' of confidence in God's power, a figure of the faith that overcomes the world (Romans 8:31; 1 John 5:4)

schools.summary: The unbelief of the ten, 'we be not able': 'but the men that went up with him said, We be not able to go up against the people; for they are stronger than we' — the ten spies contradicted Caleb, saying they were not able, for the people were stronger (the We be not able to go up against the people, the word of unbelief, the denial of God's power, the for they are stronger than we, the fear of the foe, the comparison of strength without God, the figure, the unbelief that says 'we be not able,' measuring the foe against their own strength and leaving God out, the fatal comparison, 'they are stronger than we' — true if God be not counted, but God was with them, the truth that unbelief reckons without God, and so is overcome by the obstacles, the figure of the faithless heart that looks at its own weakness and the enemy's strength and forgets the LORD, the opposite of Caleb's 'we are well able,' vv.30, 'without me ye can do nothing,' John 15:5, but 'I can do all things through Christ,' Philippians 4:13); the ten denying they were able (the word of unbelief) — the unbelief (the denial of God's power); 'they are stronger than we' (the foe measured against their own strength, God left out, Philippians 4:13, the figure, unbelief reckoning without God, the providence, the unbelief, the evil report to follow, vv.32-33) — JFB, Gill.

themes: But the men that went up with him said, We be not able to go up against the people; for they are stronger than we
the unbelief of the ten, 'we be not able'
the word of unbelief that measures the foe against their own strength and leaves God out
the opposite of Caleb's 'we are well able' (John 15:5; Philippians 4:13)

schools.summary: The evil report that slandered the land: 'and they brought up an evil report of the land which they had searched unto the children of Israel, saying, The land, through which we have gone to search it, is a land that eateth up the inhabitants thereof; and all the people that we saw in it are men of a great stature' — the ten brought up an evil (slanderous) report, calling the good land one that devours its inhabitants, and all its people of great stature (the an evil report of the land, the slander, the bad report, the a land that eateth up the inhabitants thereof, the land slandered as devouring its own, the very land they had confessed flowed with milk and honey, vv.27, the all the people... are men of a great stature, the giants, the figure, the evil report that slandered the good land, contradicting their own confession, vv.27, the unbelief that not only feared but lied, turning the good land into a devourer, the truth that unbelief becomes slander, the good gift of God maligned, the figure of the evil report that turns the people from the inheritance, the slander of God's promise, the sin that brought the forty years' judgment, vv.14:36-37, the danger of the evil report that discourages the brethren); the evil report slandering the good land (the land called a devourer, contradicting vv.27) — the slander (the good land maligned); 'all the people that we saw in it are men of a great stature' (the great stature of the people, the giants, the fear magnified, the figure, the evil report that turns the people from the inheritance, Numbers 14:36-37, the providence, the slander, the grasshoppers to follow, vv.33) — Keil and Delitzsch, Clarke, Gill.

themes: And they brought up an evil report of the land which they had searched unto the children of Israel, saying, The land... is a land that eateth up the inhabitants thereof; and all the people that we saw in it are men of a great stature
the evil report that slandered the land
the good land they had confessed now slandered as a devourer, the unbelief that not only feared but lied
the evil report that turned the people from the inheritance and brought the judgment (Numbers 14:36-37)

schools.summary: The giants and the grasshopper self-image of unbelief: 'and there we saw the giants, the sons of Anak, which come of the giants: and we were in our own sight as grasshoppers, and so were we in their sight' — the grasshopper image — 'we were in our own sight as grasshoppers, and so were we in their sight': the ten saw the giants and felt themselves as grasshoppers, small and helpless, and assumed they so appeared to the giants (the witnesses noting the significance — the self-image of unbelief, the people made small in their own eyes by the size of the obstacle, the heart that shrinks before the difficulty, the lesson — that unbelief makes the believer small and the obstacle great, the grasshopper image the very measure of their faithlessness, looking at themselves and the giants and never at God, the presumption of 'so were we in their sight' — assuming the worst, reading their own fear into the enemy, the contrast with faith — Caleb and Joshua saw the same giants but trusted God, and the giants were as bread to be eaten, 'they are bread for us... the LORD is with us: fear them not,' Numbers 14:9, the figure — the unbelief that measures itself against the obstacle and forgets the God who is greater than all, the grasshopper heart that shrinks where faith would stand, the truth that 'if God be for us, who can be against us?' Romans 8:31, and 'greater is he that is in you, than he that is in the world,' 1 John 4:4, the figure of the faithless self-image that magnifies the obstacle and minimizes the believer, the sin that turned Israel back from the inheritance and brought the forty years' wandering, the figure of unbelief that shrinks to a grasshopper before the giants); the giants, the sons of Anak (the formidable foe, the obstacle magnified) — the giants (the great obstacle); 'we were in our own sight as grasshoppers, and so were we in their sight' (the grasshopper self-image, the heart shrunk before the giants, the unbelief that made them small, Numbers 14:9, the figure, the faithless heart that forgets God, Romans 8:31; 1 John 4:4, the providence, the unbelief, the rebellion of the people to follow, ch. 14) — Calvin, Keil, Clarke, Ellicott, Gill.

debates.question: What does the grasshopper self-image reveal about unbelief?

sides.position: The self-image of unbelief that makes the believer small and the obstacle great, looking at the giants and never at God - the contrast with Caleb's faith who saw the giants as bread (Numbers 14:9), the faithless heart that forgets the God who is greater than all and brought Israel's turning back (Romans 8:31; 1 John 4:4)

themes: And there we saw the giants, the sons of Anak, which come of the giants: and we were in our own sight as grasshoppers, and so were we in their sight
the giants and the grasshopper self-image of unbelief (the crux of the unbelief that shrinks before the obstacles)
the self-image of unbelief that makes the believer small and the obstacle great, looking at the giants and never at God
the faithless heart that brought Israel's turning back and the forty years' wandering (Numbers 14:9; Romans 8:31; 1 John 4:4)
